[{"id":"rly7mqoiv6f2","name":"Mindset & Self Growth","books":[{"id":"z08e2dcpv6f2","title":"Mindset","read":true,"goat":true,"moral":"You are not what you currently are. You are what you are currently becoming — and what you are becoming is determined less by your starting point than by the belief you hold about whether your starting point is your destiny or merely your beginning.

The fixed mindset whispers that your qualities are given, that your intelligence is measured and scored, that failure reveals the truth about you, and that effort is the consolation prize of those who lack the talent to succeed without it.
Every one of these whispers is wrong.
Your intelligence is not measured — it is developing. Your talent is not given — it is being built. Your failure is not revealing a fixed truth — it is generating essential information. Your effort is not a consolation — it is the mechanism through which everything genuinely worth having is earned.
Hear the fixed mindset voice. Name it honestly. Choose differently.
Embrace the challenge. Value the effort. Learn from the criticism. Find the lesson in the failure. Celebrate the growth in others.
You are not the person you were at the beginning of the last difficult thing you attempted. You are the person that difficulty made possible.
Keep going. Keep growing. The ceiling is always higher than you think — and the only thing that makes it feel fixed is the mindset that refuses to reach for it."},{"id":"4bg5ixu8zpc4","title":"Build dont talk","read":true,"goat":true,"moral":"The world does not need more people who talk brilliantly about what they are going to build. It needs more people who build brilliantly and let the work speak for itself.

Every idea you have discussed without executing, every business you have planned without starting, every content series you have outlined without publishing, every product you have designed without shipping — these are not preparation. They are the accumulated cost of the talking trap, paid in the currency of your one irreplaceable resource: time.
Start before you are ready. Ship before it is perfect. Learn in public. Build in the open. Show up consistently. Play the long game.
Talk less. Build more.
The world is changed not by the most articulate describers of change but by the most persistent builders of it.
Stop describing. Start building. The work is waiting."},{"id":"pdvq67hlwuwe","title":"The art of impossibe","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"The motivated person is not the one who feels like working — it is the one who has built a life that makes not working feel worse than working.
Grit is a recovery skill as much as it is an endurance skill."},{"id":"ufxivzgwv6f2","title":"The Magic of Thinking Big","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"cpiano9ev6f2","title":"Think and Grow Rich","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"wmnqt8ocv6f2","title":"Who Moved My Cheese?","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"bs0l95w5v6f2","title":"The Art of Not Giving a F*ck","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"mwxsak18v6f2","title":"The Power of Now","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"wlshhv7wv6f2","title":"You Can Heal Your Life","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"543thl48v6f2","title":"The Alchemist","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"t0ctgi0f5f82","title":"Can't hurt me","read":true,"goat":true,"moral":"You are not who you think you are. You are not limited by what you have been through. You are not defined by where you started. And you are not operating anywhere near the edge of what you are actually capable of — which means the gap between your current life and your potential life is not a matter of talent, circumstance, or luck. It is a matter of the choices you make about what you are willing to suffer for.

The mind you have right now is not the mind you are stuck with. It is the mind that your choices have built — and different choices build a different mind. The evidence of your past suffering survived is already in your Cookie Jar. The 60% you have never used is already in your body, waiting for the command that never comes.
Look in the mirror. Tell the truth. Accept no comfortable excuses. Do the hard thing today so you have evidence of it tomorrow.
Apply the 40% Rule. When you feel done — continue. When you think you have reached the limit — take ten more steps. When everything says stop — ask what you are really protecting by stopping, and whether it is worth protecting at that price.
You are not your circumstances. You are not your past. You are not your feelings about what you are capable of.
You are what you do when it is hard. You are what you choose when stopping is the easier option. You are the person your suffering either makes or breaks — and that is entirely, completely, and only up to you.
Can't hurt me. Build the mind that earns the right to say it."},{"id":"08uo6w1av6f2","title":"Attitude is Everything","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"The world you experience is not the world as it is — it is the world as your attitude filters it, frames it, and ultimately creates it through the perceptions, behaviors, and relationships your attitude generates. Change the attitude and you change the world you inhabit — not because the external world changes but because your experience of it, your response to it, and your impact on it are all transformed.

The window through which you see the world is yours to clean, to adjust, and to choose. The magnetic field you broadcast is yours to upgrade. The boomerang you throw is yours to throw with intention. The commitment you make is yours to make completely. The language you speak is yours to choose carefully. The people you surround yourself with are yours to select deliberately. The fears you face are yours to approach courageously. The failures you learn from are yours to interpret generously. The persistence you maintain is yours to sustain unconditionally. The enthusiasm you bring is yours to generate authentically.
None of these require exceptional talent. None require privileged circumstances. None require fortunate timing or extraordinary opportunity.
They require only the decision — made today, renewed tomorrow, and maintained through every circumstance that tests it — that your attitude is yours to choose, that it matters more than any other single variable in your life, and that choosing it well is both your greatest responsibility and your greatest opportunity.
Your attitude is everything.
Choose it like it is.
Because it is."},{"id":"soy8wm00v6f2","title":"Believe in Yourself","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"o2kjm5efv6f2","title":"Win Your Inner Battle","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"xgm8fk18v6f2","title":"The Psychology of Winning","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"2sl565dxv6f2","title":"The Courage to Be Disliked","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"shimwlsvv6f2","title":"Ikigai","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"mq59fxu8v6f2","title":"Do Epic Shit","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"ptbsssln5nhh","title":"The Art of building a growth mindset","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"e2o6ovv37x75","title":"You are a badass","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"ubgkyrercyh3","title":"Choose Yourself","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"8xytz4n6gsas","title":"Comfortable with uncertainty","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"4ae2udpuhgry","title":"Inside the box","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"tc29epn0gs4j","title":"As a man thint it","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""}]},{"id":"9locsvvdv6f2","name":"Psychology & Thinking","books":[{"id":"rciergxov6f2","title":"Thinking, Fast and Slow","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"What gets attention gets action, regardless of actual magnitude.
You are not the impartial, rational thinker narrating your own decisions — you are the story your mind tells itself about decisions that were largely already made.

The path to better thinking is not confidence — it is calibrated humility. Question your intuitions in high-stakes, low-feedback environments. Seek the outside view before committing to plans. Reframe decisions as policies rather than individual choices. Design environ"},{"id":"2tcxxt8qv6f2","title":"Think Again","read":true,"goat":true,"moral":"Intelligence is not knowing the right answers — it is having the right relationship with being wrong.

The smartest people in any room are not those who never change their minds — they are those who change their minds most readily when the evidence demands it, without ego, without embarrassment, and without the theatrical performance of consistency that society mistakes for strength.
Rethink your beliefs before life forces you to. Question your certainties before they harden into prisons. Build"},{"id":"rvkwl1oav6f2","title":"The Intelligence Trap","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"Intelligence is the ability to process information powerfully. Wisdom is the ability to process it honestly. You need both — and the first without the second is a trap whose sophistication is proportional to its danger."},{"id":"bd5rldcev6f2","title":"Chatter","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"0ti58q18v6f2","title":"Clear Thinking","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"baw2xn3yv6f2","title":"Wiser","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"iwz1bffuv6f2","title":"Outliers","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"aphmvf0wvbg8","title":"Thinking in bets","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"f578sehsamd6","title":"The let them theory","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""}]},{"id":"51rfmxkev6f2","name":"Productivity & Discipline","books":[{"id":"5q6p4hcav6f2","title":"Atomic Habits","read":true,"goat":true,"moral":"You do not rise to the level of your goals. You fall to the level of your systems — and your systems are the sum of your habits, your environment, and the identity you are building one small behavior at a time.

The gap between who you are and who you want to become is not bridged by a single dramatic transformation. It is bridged by the daily accumulation of small decisions — each one almost invisible in isolation, collectively revolutionary over time. The 1% improvements are not inspiring. The two-minute habits are not impressive. The identity votes are not dramatic.
But the person who casts them consistently, who designs their environment deliberately, who builds their identity intentionally, and who trusts the mathematics of compounding to do its work over the time horizon that compounding requires — that person becomes, almost without noticing, someone completely different from who they started as.
Make it obvious. Make it attractive. Make it easy. Make it satisfying.
Cast the identity vote. Build the system. Trust the compound.
You are not your current habits. You are the person your next habit is building.
Start there. Start small. Start now.
And never, ever stop."},{"id":"xq853j1av6f2","title":"Getting Things Done","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"Your mind is for having ideas, not holding them. Free it — and watch what it becomes capable of.

The greatest enemy of exceptional work is not lack of talent, motivation, or time — it is the cognitive fragmentation of a mind trying to simultaneously store, schedule, worry about, and execute on hundreds of open loops without a trusted system to hold them.
Build the system. Capture everything. Clarify mercilessly. Define every next action. Review weekly without exception. Trust the system complet"},{"id":"wfsov79wv6f2","title":"Finish What You Start","read":true,"goat":true,"moral":"The world is full of brilliant beginnings that never became anything — ideas that were never developed, projects that were never completed, potential that was never realized, and contributions that were never made because the person who started them never built the system that finishing requires.

You have started things you did not finish. So has everyone. The question is not whether your past contains abandonments — it does, and so does every ambitious person's past. The question is what the pattern of your future will be, and whether you are willing to build the understanding, the systems, the habits, and the identity that make the future pattern different from the past one.
Understand why you stop. Remove the conditions that make stopping easy. Build the conditions that make continuing the path of least resistance. Automate through habit. Create accountability. Manage your energy. Navigate the middle. Push through the final resistance. Finish the imperfect thing.
Then start the next thing — with the finisher identity that the previous completion built, the system that completion validated, and the evidence that you are, in fact, someone who finishes what they start.
That evidence is the most valuable thing any completed project produces.
Build it. Protect it. Add to it. Let it compound.
Finish what you started. Then finish the next thing. Then the next.
That is the complete practice. That is the complete life."},{"id":"1ujvigamv6f2","title":"The Power of Discipline","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"c4zo8x8pv6f2","title":"The 12 Week Year","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"u2qjn476v6f2","title":"Do It Today","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"taycfhz6v6f2","title":"Deep Work","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"1yu86ubhv6f2","title":"The 5 AM Club","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"d9a5uypuv6f2","title":"The Miracle Morning","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"yst6sdduv6f2","title":"The Compound Effect","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"6yf8dadxv6f2","title":"The Practicing Mind","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"rnzkf6nv33n2","title":"Grit","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"lx11qu0f8r6t","title":"12 rules for life","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"295ps59cb3ap","title":"Essentialism","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"68g1aoyccm46","title":"The 4 Hour Work Week","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"srp40v07f7w9","title":"Multipliers","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"8o6vjd6kutlr","title":"Motivation Equation","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""}]},{"id":"ktd2xx6vv6f2","name":"Communication & Social Skills","books":[{"id":"7uk07fd2v6f2","title":"Crucial Conversations","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"The conversations you avoid are quietly shaping your life as surely as the ones you have — and usually more destructively, because avoidance leaves the situation to deteriorate on its own terms rather than improving it on yours.
Say the thing that needs to be said. Say it honestly. Say it carefully. Say it with genuine respect for the person who needs to hear it.
The conversation you have been avoiding may be the most important one of your life."},{"id":"pcxdfi84j3y7","title":"The next conversation","read":true,"goat":true,"moral":"The conversations you avoid are not protecting your relationships — they are slowly hollowing them out. The conversations you handle poorly are not expressing your true feelings — they are burying them under damage that takes far longer to repair than the honest exchange would have taken to have.

Every relationship in your life — professional, personal, romantic, familial — is built from the conversations that happened within it and shaped by the conversations that didn't. Every misunderstanding that calcified into distance, every resentment that accumulated from an unspoken boundary, every intimacy that was possible but never achieved because the threshold conversation was perpetually deferred — these are the costs of conversational avoidance and conversational skill deficits.
The skills are learnable. The mindset is cultivatable. The inner work is available to anyone willing to do it.
Choose your orientation before you speak. Choose your words with care for what they create. Say less and mean more. Set boundaries with clarity and without cruelty. Disagree with honesty and without contempt. Apologize fully and without qualification. Listen for feelings before you solve problems. Be comfortable with silence. Have the conversation you have been avoiding.
The next conversation is always an opportunity — to repair what is broken, to deepen what is shallow, to clarify what is confused, to express what is true, to honor the person in front of you with the gift of your genuine, honest, skillful presence.
Show up for it. Prepare for it. Handle it well.
Your relationships — and your life — are made of nothing else."},{"id":"q70rrvb4v6f2","title":"You're Not Listening","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"The people in your life are not failing to communicate — they are failing to be heard. And the person most responsible for that failure is not them. It is you — not from malice, not from indifference, but from the accumulated habits of distraction, performance, and the chronic busyness that has made genuine presence rare and genuine listening rarer still.

You have been in conversations where you felt the other person was genuinely listening — where their attention was so complete and their curiosity so genuine that you found yourself saying things you did not know you thought until the quality of their listening called them forth. You remember how it felt. You remember the person who made it possible.
Be that person.
Put the phone away. Quiet the Committee. Suspend the agenda. Delay the response. Follow the thread of what they are actually saying rather than the thread of what you expected them to say. Ask the question that their words make genuinely curious. Sit with the feeling before reaching for the solution. Stay present through the discomfort of perspectives that challenge your own.
Listen to understand, not to respond. Listen to the words and the body and the silence. Listen with the courage to be changed by what you hear.
The conversation you transform by genuinely listening may be the most important conversation that person has ever had.
Not because of what you said.
Because of what they finally felt able to say — to you, and to themselves — in the rare and generous space that your genuine listening made possible.
That is the gift. That is the practice. That is the art.
Listen."},{"id":"fraamjj5v6f2","title":"How to Win Friends and Influence People","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"rnx60cecv6f2","title":"How to Talk to Anyone","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"yirjgkr7v6f2","title":"Read People Like a Book","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"hvkdrm69v6f2","title":"The Art of Public Speaking","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"thpami5j0e6s","title":"Supercommunicators","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"7u4o4rzwv6f2","title":"Talk Like TED","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"v7dt664nv6f2","title":"The Culture Map","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""}]},{"id":"u3zxzi8lv6f2","name":"Money, Wealth & Finance","books":[{"id":"4w2go67lv6f2","title":"The Psychology of Money","read":true,"goat":true,"moral":"Skill and fate are inseparable in outcomes but treated as entirely separate in narratives.
Research consistently shows that control over one's time is one of the strongest predictors of happiness — stronger than income beyond a threshold, stronger than social status, stronger than most things money can buy.
The person who knows less but behaves better will always outperform the person who knows more but behaves worse
Money's greatest value is not what it buys — it is what it protects: your time,"},{"id":"iz5z5zomv6f2","title":"The Richest Man in Babylon","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"You can work hard your entire life and die poor if no one ever teaches you the laws of money.
A part of all you earn is yours to keep — and if you keep it wisely, invest it patiently, and guard it carefully, it will work for you long after you have stopped working for it.

The richest man in Babylon was not born rich. He was not luckier than his neighbors, smarter than his teachers, or more talented than his friends. He simply learned — earlier than most and applied more consistently than almost"},{"id":"qxjfa2vmue8w","title":"Invent & Wander","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"The size of your willingness to fail is the ceiling of your capacity to invent.
 You cannot select only the winning bets in advance. You can only make many thoughtful bets and learn from the ones that do not work while scaling the ones that do.
Bezos did not discover his philosophy as he built Amazon. He built Amazon from his philosophy.
The companies — and the people — that change the world are not those with the most resources, the most connections, or the most intelligence. They are those wit"},{"id":"gzdqibaqv6f2","title":"The Millionaire Fastlane","read":true,"goat":true,"moral":""},{"id":"bml7g5osv6f2","title":"Rich Dad Poor Dad","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"2kuy5eutv6f2","title":"Just Keep Buying","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"qrd0buepv6f2","title":"Main Street Millionaire","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"9ozezzxwv6f2","title":"Secret of the Millionaire Mind","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"cvt732jkv6f2","title":"The Algebra of Money","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""}]},{"id":"iovne54kv6f2","name":"Habits, Emotions & Inner Work","books":[{"id":"lq54238dv6f2","title":"Master Your Emotions","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"You are not your emotions — you are the awareness that observes them, the intelligence that interprets them, and the agency that chooses how to respond to them. Claim that awareness, develop that intelligence, and exercise that agency — and your emotional life transforms from something that happens to you into something you actively, skillfully, and compassionately create.

The emotions you have been avoiding are carrying your most important messages. The beliefs generating your most painful feelings are the ones most worth examining. The patterns you have been living unconsciously are the ones most worth bringing into the light of deliberate attention.
Feel fully. Observe honestly. Interpret carefully. Respond deliberately. Accept what cannot be changed. Change what can be.
Build the practices that create the baseline. Maintain the awareness that prevents the spirals. Develop the resilience that makes difficulty survivable. Cultivate the acceptance that makes reality bearable.
Your emotional life is not fixed. It is not fate. It is not your personality.
It is a practice — and every day is another opportunity to practice it better.
Begin with awareness. Return to awareness. Let awareness be the ground from which everything else grows.
That is the complete practice. That is the whole journey. That is the art of mastering your emotions."},{"id":"1eo8wdotv6f2","title":"Surrounded by Idiots","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"The people around you are not broken versions of you — they are complete expressions of themselves, operating from orientations as natural and as valid as yours, communicating in languages as genuine and as internally consistent as yours, and trying as hard as you are to do what seems most obviously right in the style that feels most obviously correct.

The frustration of being chronically misunderstood is real. The exhaustion of chronic communication failure is real. The genuine conviction that you are surrounded by people who do not understand simple things is real. But the diagnosis that produces these experiences is wrong — and the wrong diagnosis leads to the wrong treatment, which is finding better people, communicating more loudly in your own style, and continuing to be baffled by the same failures with different faces.
The right diagnosis is simpler, more hopeful, and more demanding: you are surrounded by people of different colors, and you have not yet learned to speak their language.
Learn the Red. Learn the Yellow. Learn the Green. Learn the Blue. Learn which one you are and what it costs those around you when you speak only in your own color. Learn to flex. Learn to adapt. Learn to appreciate the person whose style most challenges yours as the person whose contribution most completes yours.
You are not surrounded by idiots.
You are surrounded by Reds, Yellows, Greens, and Blues — each one trying, in their own language, to do exactly what you are trying to do in yours.
Learn the languages. Have the conversations. Build the teams. Deepen the relationships.
The people you once called idiots will become the most valuable people in your world — once you learn to hear what they have been saying all along."},{"id":"k77t8nu8v6f2","title":"Emotional Intelligence","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"bew422e1v6f2","title":"The Power of Positive Thinking","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"qnjqks9cv6f2","title":"The Power of Subconscious Mind","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"pg7er42dv6f2","title":"The Power of No","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"r73kz5zr6q52","title":"The art of impossible","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"m5xtpkis4trm","title":"Emotional Agility","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"6u9iwkxjd867","title":"The 5 Love Languages","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""}]},{"id":"h0ctfeylv6f2","name":"Philosophy & Deep Thinking","books":[{"id":"si9j7pbjv6f2","title":"The Law of Human Nature","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"Success is the most dangerous moment in any career — more dangerous than failure, because failure produces correction while success produces confidence that can curdle into arrogance.
A person with a clear purpose is almost impossible to stop. A person without one is almost impossible to start.
People follow leaders who make them feel secure, seen, capable, and part of something meaningful — not leaders who merely hold titles, display power, or demand compliance."},{"id":"qcracn02v6f2","title":"Being and Nothingness","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"oxdfzqm6v6f2","title":"Range","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""}]},{"id":"kug08q9ev6f2","name":"Business & Strategy","books":[{"id":"bpeqwoik5pm1","title":"Ony the paranoid survived","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"Success is the most dangerous condition a company can be in — because it generates the confidence, the organizational inertia, and the identity investment that make honest detection of change most difficult at precisely the moment when the accumulated competitive advantage makes change most possible.

The organizations that Grove profiles as having failed at strategic inflection points were not stupid, lazy, or poorly managed. They were successful — which made them invested in the strategy that had produced their success, resistant to the information that challenged it, and slow to respond to the 10X forces that were rendering it obsolete.
The paranoid organization is not anxious or reactive — it is alert, honest, and agile. It has built the cultural infrastructure to hear uncomfortable information early. It has developed the leadership capacity to act on uncomfortable information honestly. And it has maintained the organizational flexibility to redirect resources from declining opportunities to emerging ones before the crisis makes redirection unavoidable.
Monitor for 10X forces — always, in every direction, from every level of the organization. Hear your Cassandras before events vindicate them. Be willing to abandon what succeeded in order to build what will succeed next. Act before the crisis makes action feel courageous rather than obvious.
Only the paranoid survive. Not because paranoia is comfortable — but because the alternative is the comfortable complacency that 10X forces consistently and completely destroy.
Stay alert. Stay honest. Stay ready to change everything.
The inflection point is always forming. The question is whether you will see it in time."},{"id":"io8iuzrnv6f2","title":"Trillion Dollar Coach","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"b7bpznzuv6f2","title":"Good to Great","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"ep8kc4xsv6f2","title":"Zero to One","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"z2eb22knv6f2","title":"$100 Startup","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"4zvhuce0v6f2","title":"Rework","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"s1s1jrabv6f2","title":"The Challenger Sale","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""}]},{"id":"8u9r499cv6f2","name":"Trading & Investing","books":[{"id":"6ovqiuksil4i","title":"The Simple Path to Wealth","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"The path to financial freedom is not hidden behind sophisticated strategies, privileged access, or exceptional intelligence. It is available to anyone willing to spend less than they earn, invest the difference in low-cost index funds, eliminate debt, and hold everything through the market cycles that will inevitably test their resolve.

The financial industry makes its living from your complexity. Every product it sells, every advisor it employs, every fund it manages is a claim on the returns that would otherwise be yours. The single most financially empowering decision most people can make is to refuse that complexity — to own the market at minimal cost, to hold it through every storm, and to trust the two-century historical record of human productive capacity over the quarterly predictions of well-compensated professionals with financial incentives to generate activity.
Spend less than you earn. Invest the difference. Avoid debt. Stay the course.
That is the path. It is simple. It is boring. It is available to you right now, regardless of how much you earn, how much you know, or how late you think you have started.
The best time to start was twenty years ago. The second best time is today.
Start. Simplify. Hold. Be free."},{"id":"q7dcx1uuv6f2","title":"The Intelligent Investor","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"5omqoo86v6f2","title":"Market Wizards","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"uibj3zhnv6f2","title":"The Disciplined Trader","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"ah9z8w8ov6f2","title":"High Probability Trading","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"48as00jav6f2","title":"The Art of Trading","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""}]},{"id":"gbp0imhdv6f2","name":"Sales & Marketing","books":[{"id":"33coo0mwv6f2","title":"To Sell Is Human","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"The purpose of selling is not to extract something from people — it is to improve their lives and, in doing so, improve your own."},{"id":"9icnuk6gv6f2","title":"Sell to Big Companies","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"the only question that matters is "How do I help this specific company solve a specific problem?

Big companies do not buy products — they buy certainty. _allPages.habit-rings-custom-quotes: [{"text":"Starting is an emotional act. Finishing is a discipline.","author":""},{"text":"when your mind tells you that you are done, you have used approximately 40% of your actual capacity.","author":""},{"text":"Chase the becoming. Accept the trophies as receipts for the work. Never mistake the receipt for the thing itself.","author":""},{"text":"The first signal to stop is not the limit. It is the beginning of the real work.","author":""},{"text":"Pain was not a sufficient reason to stop.","author":""},{"text":"Learn to be comfortable with the pause. It is doing more work than most words.","author":""},{"text":"Behavior follows environment more reliably than it follows intention.","author":""},{"text":"The harder I work, the more luck I seem to have.","author":"Thomas Jefferson"},{"text":"Action is the foundational key to all success.","author":"Pablo Picasso"},{"text":"Motivation is what gets you started. Habit is what keeps you going.","author":"Jim Ryun"}]

{"intro":{"title":"Intro","content":"

1️⃣ Tell Me About Yourself


I'm a full-stack software engineer with 3.5 years of experience building web applications and solving business problems through technology. Currently, I work at Saxon Digital Technology, where I contribute to real-time booking platforms & third party integrations. I completed my BS in Computer Science from Karachi University, which gave me a strong foundation in software engineering principles. Over the years, I've developed expertise in the MERN stack, Datebase design, and  AI-powered solutions.

2️⃣  What Do You Do? (I help X with Y)
I help companies turn complex business ideas into scalable software systems. Most of my work involves designing backend architectures, building web applications, and integrating technologies like AI and real-time services to solve real business problems.

3️⃣ Why Do You Want to Switch?

I’m grateful for my current role because it has given me valuable experience in building production systems and solving real business problems. However, I’m now looking for opportunities to work on larger-scale architectures, learn from experienced engineers, and contribute to more complex technical challenges. I believe that environment will help me continue growing as a software engineer.

4️⃣ Why Should We Hire You / Why This Company?


You should hire me because I’m the kind of engineer who ships results. I’ve built real systems used by real users, improved performance metrics, and solved complex problems. If you want someone who learns fast, builds fast, and improves systems — I’m that person.

5️⃣ Anything Else You’d Like Me to Know?

One thing that sets me apart is curiosity. I don't just focus on completing tasks; I try to understand the architecture, business impact, and trade-offs behind technical decisions. I'm also highly adaptable and enjoy learning new technologies, which is why I've been investing time in areas like AI and system design. I believe that mindset allows me to contribute beyond my immediate responsibilities.

6️⃣  Where Do You See Yourself in 5 Years?
In five years, I see myself in a senior technical leadership role, designing scalable systems, contributing to architectural decisions, and mentoring other engineers. I want to be someone the team trusts to solve complex problems and drive technical excellence while continuing to learn and grow.

7️⃣ Challenge You Faced & Solved

One challenge I faced was optimizing document analysis speed in an AI project. Processing large files was taking too long and affecting the user experience. I redesigned the architecture by improving the parsing strategy and indexing pipeline. As a result, analysis time decreased by 80%, and accuracy improved to over 95%. That experience taught me the importance of designing for performance from the beginning.

8️⃣  Strengths & Weaknesses
Strengths
One of my biggest strengths is analytical thinking. I enjoy breaking complex problems into smaller parts and finding practical solutions. Another strength is persistence. When I face a technical challenge, I stay with the problem until I understand the root cause and resolve it. Finally, I'm disciplined in how I work, which helps me manage priorities, meet deadlines, and consistently deliver results.

Weakness
Earlier I used to try solving everything alone. Now I actively collaborate sooner, which actually improves both speed and quality.

9) Why should we choose you over another candidate with similar experience?

I believe what differentiates me is my ability to combine technical execution with business impact. I've worked on production systems where performance, scalability, and reliability directly affected users and business outcomes. I don't just focus on writing code; I focus on understanding the problem, identifying the right solution, and delivering measurable results. I'm also comfortable learning new technologies quickly and adapting to changing requirements. I believe that combination allows me to contribute value from day one.
✅ Pro Interview Tip (from HR experience):
Memorize the structure, not the script. Deliver naturally — recruiters detect memorized answers instantly.
✔️ If you want, I can also:
  • simulate a mock interview with you
  • or refine answers for FAANG / startup / remote roles
Just say mock interview.

    AI

    Start documenting your information here.

    ","subcategories":[{"id":"ai_agents","title":"AI AGENTS","content":"

    What is an AI Agent? (Plain English)

    An AI agent is a program that can:

    1. Look at a goal or question

    2. Think about what to do next

    3. Take action (search web, call an API, use a calculator, write code)

    4. Remember what it did

    5. Repeat until the goal is done

    Analogy: A robot chef. You say "Make pasta." It checks fridge (look), decides to boil water (think), turns on stove (act), tastes it (check), adds salt (adjust), serves when done.

    Compare to ChatGPT: ChatGPT just answers once. An agent can do multiple steps, use tools, and fix mistakes.

    Steps to Build an AI Agent

    Basic (Beginner)

    StepWhat you doExample
    1Pick a goal"Find today's weather in Tokyo"
    2Give the agent a toolWeather API key
    3Write a simple loopAsk LLM → decide tool → call tool → give result back
    4Stop when doneReturn weather to user

    Advanced (Experienced)

    StepWhat you add
    1Memory (short-term + long-term vector DB)
    2Multiple tools (search, code executor, database, file system)
    3Planning (agent breaks goal into subgoals before acting)
    4Reflection (agent reviews its own work and revises)
    5Multi-agent teams (one plans, one codes, one checks)
    6Observability (logging agent's every thought and action)

    Advanced stack example: LangGraph + GPT-4 + Pinecone + SerpAPI + Code interpreter

    The Tricky Part That Stumps Even Technical People

    "The agent does a lot of wrong steps before getting it right — and you can't tell if it's stuck in a loop, thinking forever, or just slow."

    Why this is hard:

    1. No guarantee of termination
      Agent says "Let me double-check that" → repeats forever. Engineers spend weeks adding loop detectors.

    2. Tool choice failure
      Agent has a calculator but tries to use the weather API for math. Fixing this requires complex prompt engineering or fine-tuning.

    3. Memory confusion
      Agent remembers wrong info from step 3 and uses it in step 10. Humans debug this like finding a needle in a haystack.

    4. Cost blow-up
      A simple question triggers 50 LLM calls → 2insteadof2insteadof0.02. Technical people forget to set budget limits.

    5. Observation vs. hallucination
      Agent says "I searched and found X" but actually never called the search tool. Very hard to catch without forced tool-use logging.

    Real example that traps devs:

    You build a research agent. It works perfectly for 5 tests. On test 6, it asks "What year is it?" → searches web → finds multiple answers → gets confused → loops between two conflicting sources → burns $15 in API costs before you kill it.

    The fix (what experts do):

    • Hard max steps (e.g., 10 actions max)

    • Step-by-step forced output format (JSON with "thought", "action", "observation")

    • Separate validator agent that checks work after every 3 steps

    🔁 Simplified Flow (What happens step-by-step)

    StepComponentWhat it does
    1User InputE.g., "Summarize latest AI news"
    2PlanningDecides: > Need to search web first, then summarize
    3RAG & ToolsSearches vector DB or web, retrieves relevant chunks
    4MemoryRemembers what was said earlier (e.g., last 5 messages)
    5LLMCombines everything → writes a summary
    6OutputFinal answer to user

    ⚠️ Challenges & Limitations (Simple List)

    ChallengeExplanation
    HallucinationLLM makes up facts that aren't in the retrieved data
    Tool misselectionAgent picks the wrong tool or wrong order of use
    Memory limitsLong conversations cause LLM to "forget" earlier parts
    LatencyMultiple steps (plan → search → retrieve → generate) = slow
    RAG qualityIf vector DB has bad data, answer will be wrong
    CostEach LLM call + tool call = $$$ (especially with large context)
    SecurityAgent might expose sensitive data to external tools
    LoopingAgent might get stuck repeating the same action infinitely

    ✅ How to Make It Even Simpler

    If you want a one-sentence version:

    An AI agent = LLM + planning + memory + tools (RAG) that takes a user request, breaks it into steps, retrieves relevant knowledge, and generates an answer.

    ","updatedAt":1779793343545},{"id":"prompt_eng","title":"Prompt Eng","content":"

    Tips

    1. Do: “Explain recursion step by step, starting with definition, then base case, then recursive case with call stack.”
      Avoid: “Explain recursion.”

    2. Do: “Use simple, everyday language and define each term you introduce.”
      Avoid: “No jargon.”

    3. Do: “Explain recursion using exactly three examples: factorial, Fibonacci, and tree traversal.”
      Avoid: “Explain recursion.”

    4. Do: “Act as a patient coding mentor for a beginner, using a warm and encouraging tone.”
      Avoid: “Explain recursion.”

    5. Do: “Explain recursion, then list two weaknesses in your answer and how you’d fix them.”
      Avoid: “Explain recursion.”

    ","updatedAt":1779793345072},{"id":"rag","title":"RAG","content":"

    📚 RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) — Complete Guide

    🔷 What is RAG?

    RAG = Retrieval (find relevant data) + Augmentation (add to prompt) + Generation (LLM produces answer)

    Core idea: Instead of asking LLM to answer from its training data (which may be outdated), you first search your knowledge base, then give LLM both the query + retrieved documents.

    🔷 Types of RAG

    TypeDescriptionWhen to use
    Naive RAGSimple retrieve → augment → generateQuick prototyping
    Hybrid RAGKeyword search + vector search togetherBetter accuracy
    Graph RAGUses knowledge graphs (relationships)Complex domains (medical, legal)
    Self-RAGLLM decides when to retrieve (not always)Reduce cost, improve relevance
    Multi-Query RAGRerank and combine multiple retrieval passesHigh precision needed
    Adaptive RAGDynamically switches retrieval strategiesProduction systems


    🔷 Best Stack Variations (2–3 Options)

    🥇 Option 1: Simple & Fast (Beginner)

    LayerChoice
    LoaderLangChain (PyPDFLoader, TextLoader)
    ChunkingRecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
    EmbeddingOpenAI text-embedding-3-small
    Vector DBChromaDB (local)
    RetrievalSimilarity search (top 3)
    LLMGPT-4o or Claude 3.5 Sonnet


    🔷 How RAG Works (2 Pipelines)

    🟢 1. DATA INGESTION PIPELINE (Offline/Setup)

    Step 1: Raw Data Sources

    • Types: PDF, Image, XLS, Text, HTML

    • Goal: Accept any document format as input

    Step 2: Parsing / Chunking

    • How to read: Use document loaders (e.g., LangChain loaders)

    • How to divide (chunking methods):

      • Fixed size: Each chunk = 500-1000 tokens

      • Semantic: Split at sentence boundaries

      • Recursive: Split while preserving document structure

    Step 3: Embedding Model

    • What it does: Convert each text chunk → vector (numerical representation)

    • Popular options:

      • OpenAI: text-embedding-3-small

      • HuggingFace: BAAI/bge-large

      • Other: Cohere, Mistral

    Step 4: Vector Database

    • What it stores: (chunk_text, vector) pairs

    • Popular options:

      • Local: ChromaDB

      • Cloud: Pinecone

      • Other: Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus

    🟢 2. RETRIEVAL PIPELINE (Online / Runtime)

    Step 1: User Query

        • Input: Natural language question (e.g., "What is the ROI of AI in healthcare?")

    Step 2: Query Embedding

        • Process: Convert user question → vector

        • Requirement: Use the same embedding model as ingestion

    Step 3: Similarity Search

        • Goal: Compare query vector to all vectors in database

        • Algorithms:

          • Cosine similarity

          • Euclidean distance

          • Dot product

        • Output: Return top-k most similar chunks

    Step 4: Augmentation (Context)

        • Combine: Retrieved chunks + original user query

        • Prompt structure:

          text

          "Answer using ONLY this text:
 [chunks]
 Question: [query]"

    Step 5: Generation (LLM)

          • What it does: LLM produces grounded answer using only provided context

          • LLM options: GPT-4, Claude, Llama 3

    Step 6: Output

          • Final answer delivered to user

    🔄 Summary Flow (Both Pipelines Combined)

          • PhaseInputProcessOutput
            IngestionRaw documentsParse → Chunk → Embed → StoreVector DB
            RetrievalUser queryEmbed → Similarity search → Augment → GenerateAnswer

    ","updatedAt":1778534241167},{"id":"mcp","title":"MCP","content":"

    🧠 What is MCP?

    • Model Context Protocol — open standard by Anthropic

    • Connects AI/LLMs to external tools and data

    • Like USB-C for AI — one protocol works for everything

    ❓ Problem Solved

    • Isolation — AI only knows its training data

    • Fragmentation — Each tool used to need custom code

    • No real-world access — AI couldn't read files, use APIs, or modify data

    🏠 Simple Analogy

    • AI = smart person locked in a room (can think but can't act)

    • MCP = key to unlock the room (gives AI eyes, hands, legs)

    🔌 Core Components (Easy Version)

    ComponentWhat it does
    MCP HostThe AI app (Claude, Cursor)
    MCP ServerAdapter that connects a tool (files, database, web)
    ResourcesData the AI can read
    ToolsActions the AI can do (write, send, delete)

    💪 What Can You Do with MCP?

    • Read files on your computer

    • Query databases

    • Check weather / stock prices

    • Send emails or Slack messages

    • Search the web

    • Update calendars

    • Edit documents

    🛠️ How to Build (Simple)

    1. Write a small server (Python, JavaScript, etc.)

    2. Define tools (read file, send email, search web)

    3. Run the server

    4. Connect to MCP host (Claude Desktop, Cursor)

    🚧 Challenges

    ProblemSimple Explanation
    SecurityMCP can expose sensitive files if not careful
    CostEach MCP call = money (API tokens, LLM costs)
    SpeedAI has to think → search → think again = slow
    AdoptionStill new; not all AI tools support it

    ✅ One Line Summary

    MCP gives AI real-world power by connecting it to files, apps, and the internet through one standard protocol.

    ","updatedAt":1779793346281},{"id":"automation","title":"Automation","content":"

    🧠 What is AI Automation?

    AI Automation = Using AI to decide, act, and adapt in workflows — not just follow fixed rules.

    Traditional AutomationAI Automation
    If X → do Y (hardcoded)If X → AI decides Y or Z or A
    No intelligenceContext-aware decisions
    Breaks when input changesAdapts to new inputs

    🏗️ How to Build Workflows (3 Layers)

    Layer 1: Triggers (When to start?)

    • Schedule (every morning)

    • Event (new email, file upload, webhook)

    • Manual (user clicks button)

    Layer 2: Actions (What to do?)

    • AI decides actions based on context

    • Static actions (send email, update DB, call API)

    • Conditional logic (if X → do A, else do B)

    Layer 3: Orchestration (Who runs it?)

    • n8n — Self-hosted visual workflow builder

    • Zapier — Cloud-based, no-code, 6000+ apps

    • Custom AI Agents — Code-based, full control

    🔧 n8n vs Zapier vs Custom AI Agents

    Featuren8nZapierCustom AI Agent
    HostingSelf-hosted (your server)Cloud (Zapier's servers)Anywhere (you control)
    CostFree (self-host)Paid (starts ~$20/mo)Free + cloud API costs
    AI integrationNative (LangChain, OpenAI)Limited (AI actions)Full control (any LLM)
    ComplexityMedium (visual + code)Low (point-click)High (code required)
    Data privacy✅ Full control❌ Data flows through Zapier✅ Full control
    App connectors400+6000+Unlimited (you build)
    Best forInternal business automationMarketing / personal useCustom agent workflows

    🎯 When to Use Which?

    ✅ Use n8n when:

    • You need privacy (sensitive data)

    • You have technical skills (or a dev team)

    • You need complex logic (loops, error handling, retries)

    • You want one-time cost (self-hosted free)

    ✅ Use Zapier when:

    • You need no-code (non-technical team)

    • You need quick integration (6000+ pre-built connectors)

    • You don't care about data privacy

    • You have simple workflows (if this → then that)

    ✅ Use Custom AI Agent when:

    • You need full control (custom logic, memory, tools)

    • You need multi-step reasoning (not just fixed actions)

    • You have LLM integration (RAG, planning, tool use)

    • You want complex AI behavior (self-correction, reflection)

        WHY THESE WORK:
        These are “save-worthy” topics.

        TIER 2 — AUTHORITY BUILDING TOPICS

        (Post these 20–30% of the time)

        These topics build deep credibility.

        They attract:
        • Senior engineers
        • Tech leads
        • Recruiters
        • Serious developers

        Best Topics

        • Saga Pattern

        • CQRS & Event Sourcing

        • CI/CD for Distributed Systems

        WHY THESE WORK:
        These topics won’t always go viral.
        But they position you as someone who actually understands engineering deeply.

        TIER 3 — PERSONAL BRAND + REAL TALK CONTENT

        (Post these 10–20% of the time)

    Question post

    1. Why do intelligent people sometimes make terrible life decisions?

    Good Answer:

    "Intelligence alone is not enough to make good decisions. Many decisions are influenced by emotions, pressure, and personal experiences. Even intelligent people can make poor choices when emotions affect their judgment."

    2. Why do many people quit too early instead of staying consistent?

    Good Answer:

    "Many people quit too early because progress is often invisible in the beginning. When they do not see immediate results, they lose motivation and stop putting in effort."

    3. What is one habit that silently destroys people's potential?

    Good Answer:

    "One habit that silently destroys people's potential is a lack of focus. Many people try too many things at the same time instead of mastering one skill properly."

    4. What is the difference between being busy and being productive?

    Good Answer:

    "Being busy means constantly doing tasks without producing meaningful results. Being productive means focusing on work that actually moves you closer to your goals."

    5. Why do some talented people never become successful?

    Good Answer:

    "Some talented people never become successful because talent alone is not enough. Success also requires consistency, discipline, and continuous effort."

    6. What is more dangerous: lack of discipline or lack of self-awareness?

    Good Answer:

    "I believe a lack of discipline is more dangerous because a person may understand their weaknesses clearly, but without discipline they will not take action to improve."

    7. What is the difference between confidence and competence?

    Good Answer:

    "Confidence is the belief that you can do something successfully, while competence is the actual ability to do it well. Confidence creates a strong impression, but competence creates real value."

    8. Why do some people avoid taking responsibility for their mistakes?

    Good Answer:

    "Some people avoid taking responsibility because they are afraid of criticism, failure, or damage to their self-image. Blaming others often feels easier than accepting fault."

    9. Why do many people seek validation from others?

    Good Answer:

    "Many people seek validation because their self-worth depends heavily on other people's opinions and approval."

    10. Why do some people become arrogant after achieving success?

    Good Answer:

    "Some people become arrogant because success increases their ego. They may begin to believe they are superior to others and stop listening to feedback."

    11. Why do many people know what is right but still fail to do it?

    Good Answer:

    "There is a big difference between knowing something and doing it. Many people understand what should be done, but very few take consistent action."

    12. Why do some people stay in their comfort zone even when it hurts their growth?

    Good Answer:

    "People stay in their comfort zone because it feels safe and familiar. Growth often requires uncertainty and discomfort, which many people try to avoid."

    13. Why do some people never feel satisfied even after achieving success?

    Good Answer:

    "Some people never feel satisfied because their expectations continue to increase. Instead of appreciating their progress, they focus only on what they still lack."

    14. Why do some people avoid honest feedback?

    Good Answer:

    "Some people avoid honest feedback because criticism makes them uncomfortable. They often take feedback personally instead of viewing it as an opportunity to improve."

    15. Why do some people seek distractions instead of facing their problems?

    Good Answer:

    "Some people seek distractions because facing real problems can be emotionally uncomfortable. Distractions provide temporary relief from that discomfort."

    16. Why do some people repeat the same mistakes?

    Good Answer:

    "Some people repeat the same mistakes because they fail to reflect on their behavior and identify the root cause of the problem."

    17. Why do some people give advice easily but fail to follow it themselves?

    Good Answer:

    "It is easier to think logically about someone else's situation than your own. Emotions often make it difficult to follow your own advice."

    18. Why do some people keep making excuses?

    Good Answer:

    "People make excuses because excuses are easier than accepting responsibility. Excuses provide temporary emotional comfort."

    19. Why do some people wait for the perfect time?

    Good Answer:

    "People wait for the perfect time because they want certainty before taking action. They fear making mistakes or facing failure."

    20. Why do some people get offended by criticism?

    Good Answer:

    "Some people get offended because they see criticism as a personal attack rather than useful feedback."

    21. Why do some people blame circumstances for everything?

    Good Answer:

    "Some people blame circumstances because accepting responsibility can be uncomfortable and threatening to their self-image."

    22. Why do some people become jealous when others succeed?

    Good Answer:

    "People become jealous when they compare someone else's success to their own situation and feel left behind."

    23. Why do some people refuse to change their opinion even when evidence proves they are wrong?

    Good Answer:

    "Some people refuse to change their opinion because admitting they are wrong threatens their ego and identity."

    24. Why do some people constantly seek attention?

    Good Answer:

    "Some people seek attention because their self-worth depends heavily on recognition and validation from others."

    25. Why do some people become obsessed with proving others wrong?

    Good Answer:

    "Some people become obsessed with proving others wrong because they tie their self-worth to being right."

    26. Why do some people feel uncomfortable when someone else is more successful than they are?

    Good Answer:

    "They compare themselves to others and interpret another person's success as evidence of their own

    7 prompts that turn a profile into one recruiters cannot scroll past:

    ● The Headline Audit

    "My current LinkedIn headline is: [paste]. I work in [industry] targeting [role type]. Generate 10 headline rewrites ranked most conservative to most aggressive. Each must communicate a specific result I delivered, not a job title. Show what makes each version work and which recruiter it appeals to."

    ✅ The first 220 characters decide whether a recruiter clicks. Most people fill them with the wrong information.

    ● The About Section Build

    "Rewrite my About section as a 3-paragraph story. Paragraph 1: the specific problem I solve. Paragraph 2: proof, with numbers. Paragraph 3: who I want to hear from and how to reach me. Under 1,800 characters. First 2 lines must hook someone with 4 seconds."

    ✅ The About section is where you control the entire narrative. Most people use it to repeat their resume.

    ● The Experience Rewrite

    "For each of my last 3 jobs, rewrite using: an opening sentence stating the business problem I owned, 3 bullets each with a measurable outcome, and a closing sentence on what changed because of my work. Replace every verb like 'managed' with one that shows ownership. Add numbers wherever the data supports them."

    ✅ Recruiters scan for numbers. No numbers, no callback.

    ● The Search Optimization Pass

    "Pretend you're a senior recruiter searching LinkedIn for a [role] at a [company size/type]. List the exact 25 keywords you'd type into the search bar. Tell me which are missing from my profile, which sections to add them to, and how without keyword stuffing."

    ✅ LinkedIn is a search engine. Your profile either matches what recruiters type or it doesn't exist to them.

    ● The Brutal Review

    "Read my full LinkedIn profile as a senior recruiter at [target company] with 50 other profiles to review. Give me the 30-second verdict: reach out, save, or skip? List the 5 things hurting the profile most and the 3 things that work. No softening."

    ✅ Most people have never seen their profile the way a recruiter actually does.

    The last 2 prompts are in the comments.

    LinkedIn Premium tells you who viewed your profile
    Claude does the work that gets recruiters to view it

    Save these.
    If someone tells you LinkedIn is dead, send them this.

[{"id":"rly7mqoiv6f2","name":"Mindset & Self Growth","books":[{"id":"z08e2dcpv6f2","title":"Mindset","read":true,"goat":true,"moral":"You are not what you currently are. You are what you are currently becoming — and what you are becoming is determined less by your starting point than by the belief you hold about whether your starting point is your destiny or merely your beginning.

The fixed mindset whispers that your qualities are given, that your intelligence is measured and scored, that failure reveals the truth about you, and that effort is the consolation prize of those who lack the talent to succeed without it.
Every one of these whispers is wrong.
Your intelligence is not measured — it is developing. Your talent is not given — it is being built. Your failure is not revealing a fixed truth — it is generating essential information. Your effort is not a consolation — it is the mechanism through which everything genuinely worth having is earned.
Hear the fixed mindset voice. Name it honestly. Choose differently.
Embrace the challenge. Value the effort. Learn from the criticism. Find the lesson in the failure. Celebrate the growth in others.
You are not the person you were at the beginning of the last difficult thing you attempted. You are the person that difficulty made possible.
Keep going. Keep growing. The ceiling is always higher than you think — and the only thing that makes it feel fixed is the mindset that refuses to reach for it."},{"id":"4bg5ixu8zpc4","title":"Build dont talk","read":true,"goat":true,"moral":"The world does not need more people who talk brilliantly about what they are going to build. It needs more people who build brilliantly and let the work speak for itself.

Every idea you have discussed without executing, every business you have planned without starting, every content series you have outlined without publishing, every product you have designed without shipping — these are not preparation. They are the accumulated cost of the talking trap, paid in the currency of your one irreplaceable resource: time.
Start before you are ready. Ship before it is perfect. Learn in public. Build in the open. Show up consistently. Play the long game.
Talk less. Build more.
The world is changed not by the most articulate describers of change but by the most persistent builders of it.
Stop describing. Start building. The work is waiting."},{"id":"pdvq67hlwuwe","title":"The art of impossibe","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"The motivated person is not the one who feels like working — it is the one who has built a life that makes not working feel worse than working.
Grit is a recovery skill as much as it is an endurance skill."},{"id":"ufxivzgwv6f2","title":"The Magic of Thinking Big","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"cpiano9ev6f2","title":"Think and Grow Rich","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"wmnqt8ocv6f2","title":"Who Moved My Cheese?","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"bs0l95w5v6f2","title":"The Art of Not Giving a F*ck","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"mwxsak18v6f2","title":"The Power of Now","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"wlshhv7wv6f2","title":"You Can Heal Your Life","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"543thl48v6f2","title":"The Alchemist","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"t0ctgi0f5f82","title":"Can't hurt me","read":true,"goat":true,"moral":"You are not who you think you are. You are not limited by what you have been through. You are not defined by where you started. And you are not operating anywhere near the edge of what you are actually capable of — which means the gap between your current life and your potential life is not a matter of talent, circumstance, or luck. It is a matter of the choices you make about what you are willing to suffer for.

The mind you have right now is not the mind you are stuck with. It is the mind that your choices have built — and different choices build a different mind. The evidence of your past suffering survived is already in your Cookie Jar. The 60% you have never used is already in your body, waiting for the command that never comes.
Look in the mirror. Tell the truth. Accept no comfortable excuses. Do the hard thing today so you have evidence of it tomorrow.
Apply the 40% Rule. When you feel done — continue. When you think you have reached the limit — take ten more steps. When everything says stop — ask what you are really protecting by stopping, and whether it is worth protecting at that price.
You are not your circumstances. You are not your past. You are not your feelings about what you are capable of.
You are what you do when it is hard. You are what you choose when stopping is the easier option. You are the person your suffering either makes or breaks — and that is entirely, completely, and only up to you.
Can't hurt me. Build the mind that earns the right to say it."},{"id":"08uo6w1av6f2","title":"Attitude is Everything","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"The world you experience is not the world as it is — it is the world as your attitude filters it, frames it, and ultimately creates it through the perceptions, behaviors, and relationships your attitude generates. Change the attitude and you change the world you inhabit — not because the external world changes but because your experience of it, your response to it, and your impact on it are all transformed.

The window through which you see the world is yours to clean, to adjust, and to choose. The magnetic field you broadcast is yours to upgrade. The boomerang you throw is yours to throw with intention. The commitment you make is yours to make completely. The language you speak is yours to choose carefully. The people you surround yourself with are yours to select deliberately. The fears you face are yours to approach courageously. The failures you learn from are yours to interpret generously. The persistence you maintain is yours to sustain unconditionally. The enthusiasm you bring is yours to generate authentically.
None of these require exceptional talent. None require privileged circumstances. None require fortunate timing or extraordinary opportunity.
They require only the decision — made today, renewed tomorrow, and maintained through every circumstance that tests it — that your attitude is yours to choose, that it matters more than any other single variable in your life, and that choosing it well is both your greatest responsibility and your greatest opportunity.
Your attitude is everything.
Choose it like it is.
Because it is."},{"id":"soy8wm00v6f2","title":"Believe in Yourself","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"o2kjm5efv6f2","title":"Win Your Inner Battle","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"xgm8fk18v6f2","title":"The Psychology of Winning","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"2sl565dxv6f2","title":"The Courage to Be Disliked","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"shimwlsvv6f2","title":"Ikigai","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"mq59fxu8v6f2","title":"Do Epic Shit","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"ptbsssln5nhh","title":"The Art of building a growth mindset","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"e2o6ovv37x75","title":"You are a badass","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"ubgkyrercyh3","title":"Choose Yourself","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"8xytz4n6gsas","title":"Comfortable with uncertainty","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"4ae2udpuhgry","title":"Inside the box","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""},{"id":"tc29epn0gs4j","title":"As a man thint it","read":false,"goat":false,"moral":""}]},{"id":"9locsvvdv6f2","name":"Psychology & Thinking","books":[{"id":"rciergxov6f2","title":"Thinking, Fast and Slow","read":true,"goat":false,"moral":"What gets attention gets action, regardless of actual magnitude.
You are not the impartial, rational thinker narrating your own decisions — you are the story your mind tells itself about decisions that were largely already made.

The path to better thinking is not confidence — it is calibrated humility. Question your intuitions in high-stakes, low-feedback environments. Seek the outside view before committing to plans. Reframe decisions as policies rather than individual choices. Design environ"},{"id":"2tcxxt8qv6f2","title":"Think Again","read":true,"goat":true,"moral":"Intelligence is not knowing the right answers — it is having the right relationship with being wrong.

The smartest people in any room are not those who never change their minds — they are those who change their minds most readily when the evidence demands it, without ego, without embarrassment, and without the theatrical performance of consistency that society mistakes for strength.
