    LLM API Integration, AI Agents, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), Model Context Protocol (MCP), Agentic IDEs, Prompt Engineering, Vector Databases (Chroma, Pinecone), Langchain

    Blockchain Development
    Solidity, Smart Contracts, Hardhat, Truffle, Ganache, MetaMask, Remix

    Tools & Technologies
    Firebase, Jest, Socket.io, Jira, Postman, APM Tools, Payment Gateway Integrations (Stripe)
    AI Development Tools: Cursor, codex, Claude code 

    CORE STRENGTHS

    • Adaptability & Flexibility in fast-changing environments
    • Strategic Thinking with an efficiency-first mindset

subcategories.title: AI AGENTS
subcategories.content:
What is an AI Agent? (Plain English)

An AI agent is a program that can:

  1. Look at a goal or question

  2. Think about what to do next

  3. Take action (search web, call an API, use a calculator, write code)

  4. Remember what it did

  5. Repeat until the goal is done

Analogy: A robot chef. You say "Make pasta." It checks fridge (look), decides to boil water (think), turns on stove (act), tastes it (check), adds salt (adjust), serves when done.

Compare to ChatGPT: ChatGPT just answers once. An agent can do multiple steps, use tools, and fix mistakes.

Steps to Build an AI Agent

Basic (Beginner)

StepWhat you doExample
1Pick a goal"Find today's weather in Tokyo"
2Give the agent a toolWeather API key
3Write a simple loopAsk LLM → decide tool → call tool → give result back
4Stop when doneReturn weather to user

Advanced (Experienced)

StepWhat you add
1Memory (short-term + long-term vector DB)
2Multiple tools (search, code executor, database, file system)
3Planning (agent breaks goal into subgoals before acting)
4Reflection (agent reviews its own work and revises)
5Multi-agent teams (one plans, one codes, one checks)
6Observability (logging agent's every thought and action)

Advanced stack example: LangGraph + GPT-4 + Pinecone + SerpAPI + Code interpreter

The Tricky Part That Stumps Even Technical People

"The agent does a lot of wrong steps before getting it right — and you can't tell if it's stuck in a loop, thinking forever, or just slow."

Why this is hard:

  1. No guarantee of termination
    Agent says "Let me double-check that" → repeats forever. Engineers spend weeks adding loop detectors.

  2. Tool choice failure
    Agent has a calculator but tries to use the weather API for math. Fixing this requires complex prompt engineering or fine-tuning.

  3. Memory confusion
    Agent remembers wrong info from step 3 and uses it in step 10. Humans debug this like finding a needle in a haystack.

  4. Cost blow-up
    A simple question triggers 50 LLM calls → 2insteadof2insteadof0.02. Technical people forget to set budget limits.

  5. Observation vs. hallucination
    Agent says "I searched and found X" but actually never called the search tool. Very hard to catch without forced tool-use logging.

Real example that traps devs:

You build a research agent. It works perfectly for 5 tests. On test 6, it asks "What year is it?" → searches web → finds multiple answers → gets confused → loops between two conflicting sources → burns $15 in API costs before you kill it.

The fix (what experts do):

  • Hard max steps (e.g., 10 actions max)

  • Step-by-step forced output format (JSON with "thought", "action", "observation")

  • Separate validator agent that checks work after every 3 steps

🔁 Simplified Flow (What happens step-by-step)

StepComponentWhat it does
1User InputE.g., "Summarize latest AI news"
2PlanningDecides: > Need to search web first, then summarize
3RAG & ToolsSearches vector DB or web, retrieves relevant chunks
4MemoryRemembers what was said earlier (e.g., last 5 messages)
5LLMCombines everything → writes a summary
6OutputFinal answer to user

⚠️ Challenges & Limitations (Simple List)

ChallengeExplanation
HallucinationLLM makes up facts that aren't in the retrieved data
Tool misselectionAgent picks the wrong tool or wrong order of use
Memory limitsLong conversations cause LLM to "forget" earlier parts
LatencyMultiple steps (plan → search → retrieve → generate) = slow
RAG qualityIf vector DB has bad data, answer will be wrong
CostEach LLM call + tool call = $$$ (especially with large context)
SecurityAgent might expose sensitive data to external tools
LoopingAgent might get stuck repeating the same action infinitely

✅ How to Make It Even Simpler

If you want a one-sentence version:

An AI agent = LLM + planning + memory + tools (RAG) that takes a user request, breaks it into steps, retrieves relevant knowledge, and generates an answer.

subcategories.title: Prompt Eng
subcategories.content:
Tips

  1. Do: “Explain recursion step by step, starting with definition, then base case, then recursive case with call stack.”
    Avoid: “Explain recursion.”

  2. Do: “Use simple, everyday language and define each term you introduce.”
    Avoid: “No jargon.”

  3. Do: “Explain recursion using exactly three examples: factorial, Fibonacci, and tree traversal.”
    Avoid: “Explain recursion.”

  4. Do: “Act as a patient coding mentor for a beginner, using a warm and encouraging tone.”
    Avoid: “Explain recursion.”

  5. Do: “Explain recursion, then list two weaknesses in your answer and how you’d fix them.”
    Avoid: “Explain recursion.”

subcategories.title: RAG
subcategories.content:
📚 RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) — Complete Guide

🔷 What is RAG?

RAG = Retrieval (find relevant data) + Augmentation (add to prompt) + Generation (LLM produces answer)

Core idea: Instead of asking LLM to answer from its training data (which may be outdated), you first search your knowledge base, then give LLM both the query + retrieved documents.

🔷 Types of RAG

TypeDescriptionWhen to use
Naive RAGSimple retrieve → augment → generateQuick prototyping
Hybrid RAGKeyword search + vector search togetherBetter accuracy
Graph RAGUses knowledge graphs (relationships)Complex domains (medical, legal)
Self-RAGLLM decides when to retrieve (not always)Reduce cost, improve relevance
Multi-Query RAGRerank and combine multiple retrieval passesHigh precision needed
Adaptive RAGDynamically switches retrieval strategiesProduction systems


🔷 Best Stack Variations (2–3 Options)

🥇 Option 1: Simple & Fast (Beginner)

LayerChoice
LoaderLangChain (PyPDFLoader, TextLoader)
ChunkingRecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
EmbeddingOpenAI text-embedding-3-small
Vector DBChromaDB (local)
RetrievalSimilarity search (top 3)
LLMGPT-4o or Claude 3.5 Sonnet


🔷 How RAG Works (2 Pipelines)

🟢 1. DATA INGESTION PIPELINE (Offline/Setup)

Step 1: Raw Data Sources

  • Types: PDF, Image, XLS, Text, HTML

  • Goal: Accept any document format as input

Step 2: Parsing / Chunking

  • How to read: Use document loaders (e.g., LangChain loaders)

  • How to divide (chunking methods):

    • Fixed size: Each chunk = 500-1000 tokens

    • Semantic: Split at sentence boundaries

    • Recursive: Split while preserving document structure

Step 3: Embedding Model

  • What it does: Convert each text chunk → vector (numerical representation)

  • Popular options:

    • OpenAI: text-embedding-3-small

    • HuggingFace: BAAI/bge-large

    • Other: Cohere, Mistral

Step 4: Vector Database

  • What it stores: (chunk_text, vector) pairs

  • Popular options:

    • Local: ChromaDB

    • Cloud: Pinecone

    • Other: Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus

🟢 2. RETRIEVAL PIPELINE (Online / Runtime)

Step 1: User Query

      • Input: Natural language question (e.g., "What is the ROI of AI in healthcare?")

Step 2: Query Embedding

      • Process: Convert user question → vector

      • Requirement: Use the same embedding model as ingestion

Step 3: Similarity Search

      • Goal: Compare query vector to all vectors in database

      • Algorithms:

        • Cosine similarity

        • Euclidean distance

        • Dot product

      • Output: Return top-k most similar chunks

Step 4: Augmentation (Context)

      • Combine: Retrieved chunks + original user query

      • Prompt structure:

        text

        "Answer using ONLY this text:
 [chunks]
 Question: [query]"

Step 5: Generation (LLM)

        • What it does: LLM produces grounded answer using only provided context

        • LLM options: GPT-4, Claude, Llama 3

Step 6: Output

        • Final answer delivered to user

🔄 Summary Flow (Both Pipelines Combined)

        • PhaseInputProcessOutput
          IngestionRaw documentsParse → Chunk → Embed → StoreVector DB
          RetrievalUser queryEmbed → Similarity search → Augment → GenerateAnswer

subcategories.title: MCP
subcategories.content:
🧠 What is MCP?

  • Model Context Protocol — open standard by Anthropic

  • Connects AI/LLMs to external tools and data

  • Like USB-C for AI — one protocol works for everything

❓ Problem Solved

  • Isolation — AI only knows its training data

  • Fragmentation — Each tool used to need custom code

  • No real-world access — AI couldn't read files, use APIs, or modify data

🏠 Simple Analogy

  • AI = smart person locked in a room (can think but can't act)

  • MCP = key to unlock the room (gives AI eyes, hands, legs)

🔌 Core Components (Easy Version)

ComponentWhat it does
MCP HostThe AI app (Claude, Cursor)
MCP ServerAdapter that connects a tool (files, database, web)
ResourcesData the AI can read
ToolsActions the AI can do (write, send, delete)

💪 What Can You Do with MCP?

  • Read files on your computer

  • Query databases

  • Check weather / stock prices

  • Send emails or Slack messages

  • Search the web

  • Update calendars

  • Edit documents

🛠️ How to Build (Simple)

  1. Write a small server (Python, JavaScript, etc.)

  2. Define tools (read file, send email, search web)

  3. Run the server

  4. Connect to MCP host (Claude Desktop, Cursor)

🚧 Challenges

ProblemSimple Explanation
SecurityMCP can expose sensitive files if not careful
CostEach MCP call = money (API tokens, LLM costs)
SpeedAI has to think → search → think again = slow
AdoptionStill new; not all AI tools support it

✅ One Line Summary

MCP gives AI real-world power by connecting it to files, apps, and the internet through one standard protocol.

subcategories.title: Automation
subcategories.content:
🧠 What is AI Automation?

AI Automation = Using AI to decide, act, and adapt in workflows — not just follow fixed rules.

Traditional AutomationAI Automation
If X → do Y (hardcoded)If X → AI decides Y or Z or A
No intelligenceContext-aware decisions
Breaks when input changesAdapts to new inputs

🏗️ How to Build Workflows (3 Layers)

Layer 1: Triggers (When to start?)

  • Schedule (every morning)

  • Event (new email, file upload, webhook)

  • Manual (user clicks button)

Layer 2: Actions (What to do?)

  • AI decides actions based on context

  • Static actions (send email, update DB, call API)

  • Conditional logic (if X → do A, else do B)

Layer 3: Orchestration (Who runs it?)

  • n8n — Self-hosted visual workflow builder

  • Zapier — Cloud-based, no-code, 6000+ apps

  • Custom AI Agents — Code-based, full control

🔧 n8n vs Zapier vs Custom AI Agents

Featuren8nZapierCustom AI Agent
HostingSelf-hosted (your server)Cloud (Zapier's servers)Anywhere (you control)
CostFree (self-host)Paid (starts ~$20/mo)Free + cloud API costs
AI integrationNative (LangChain, OpenAI)Limited (AI actions)Full control (any LLM)
ComplexityMedium (visual + code)Low (point-click)High (code required)
Data privacy✅ Full control❌ Data flows through Zapier✅ Full control
App connectors400+6000+Unlimited (you build)
Best forInternal business automationMarketing / personal useCustom agent workflows

🎯 When to Use Which?

✅ Use n8n when:

  • You need privacy (sensitive data)

  • You have technical skills (or a dev team)

  • You need complex logic (loops, error handling, retries)

  • You want one-time cost (self-hosted free)

✅ Use Zapier when:

  • You need no-code (non-technical team)

  • You need quick integration (6000+ pre-built connectors)

  • You don't care about data privacy

  • You have simple workflows (if this → then that)

✅ Use Custom AI Agent when:

  • You need full control (custom logic, memory, tools)

  • You need multi-step reasoning (not just fixed actions)

  • You have LLM integration (RAG, planning, tool use)

  • You want complex AI behavior (self-correction, reflection)

      People repost them because they make engineers look smarter.
      This is where your follower growth will explode.

      TIER 2 — AUTHORITY BUILDING TOPICS

      (Post these 20–30% of the time)

      These topics build deep credibility.

      They attract:
      • Senior engineers
      • Tech leads

      But they position you as someone who actually understands engineering deeply.

      TIER 3 — PERSONAL BRAND + REAL TALK CONTENT

      (Post these 10–20% of the time)

      This is what creates loyal followers.

      This is where people emotionally connect with you.

      WHY THESE WORK:
      This content creates comments.
      Comments create reach.
      Reach builds brand.

      YOUR BEST CONTENT FORMULA

      The BEST strategy for you is:

      70% → Visual technical breakdowns
      20% → Deep engineering insights
      10% → Raw real-talk mindset posts

      That combination is extremely powerful.

      Because:
      Most creators only motivate.
      Most creators only teach.
      Very few can do BOTH.

      That’s your advantage.

      YOUR HIGHEST POTENTIAL CONTENT SERIES

subcategories.title: Question post
subcategories.content:
Question post

1. Why do intelligent people sometimes make terrible life decisions?

Good Answer:

"Intelligence alone is not enough to make good decisions. Many decisions are influenced by emotions, pressure, and personal experiences. Even intelligent people can make poor choices when emotions affect their judgment."

2. Why do many people quit too early instead of staying consistent?

Good Answer:

"Many people quit too early because progress is often invisible in the beginning. When they do not see immediate results, they lose motivation and stop putting in effort."

3. What is one habit that silently destroys people's potential?

Good Answer:

"One habit that silently destroys people's potential is a lack of focus. Many people try too many things at the same time instead of mastering one skill properly."

4. What is the difference between being busy and being productive?

Good Answer:

"Being busy means constantly doing tasks without producing meaningful results. Being productive means focusing on work that actually moves you closer to your goals."

5. Why do some talented people never become successful?

Good Answer:

"Some talented people never become successful because talent alone is not enough. Success also requires consistency, discipline, and continuous effort."

6. What is more dangerous: lack of discipline or lack of self-awareness?

Good Answer:

"I believe a lack of discipline is more dangerous because a person may understand their weaknesses clearly, but without discipline they will not take action to improve."

7. What is the difference between confidence and competence?

Good Answer:

"Confidence is the belief that you can do something successfully, while competence is the actual ability to do it well. Confidence creates a strong impression, but competence creates real value."

8. Why do some people avoid taking responsibility for their mistakes?

Good Answer:

"Some people avoid taking responsibility because they are afraid of criticism, failure, or damage to their self-image. Blaming others often feels easier than accepting fault."

9. Why do many people seek validation from others?

Good Answer:

"Many people seek validation because their self-worth depends heavily on other people's opinions and approval."

10. Why do some people become arrogant after achieving success?

Good Answer:

"Some people become arrogant because success increases their ego. They may begin to believe they are superior to others and stop listening to feedback."

11. Why do many people know what is right but still fail to do it?

Good Answer:

"There is a big difference between knowing something and doing it. Many people understand what should be done, but very few take consistent action."

12. Why do some people stay in their comfort zone even when it hurts their growth?

Good Answer:

"People stay in their comfort zone because it feels safe and familiar. Growth often requires uncertainty and discomfort, which many people try to avoid."

13. Why do some people never feel satisfied even after achieving success?

Good Answer:

"Some people never feel satisfied because their expectations continue to increase. Instead of appreciating their progress, they focus only on what they still lack."

14. Why do some people avoid honest feedback?

Good Answer:

"Some people avoid honest feedback because criticism makes them uncomfortable. They often take feedback personally instead of viewing it as an opportunity to improve."

15. Why do some people seek distractions instead of facing their problems?

Good Answer:

"Some people seek distractions because facing real problems can be emotionally uncomfortable. Distractions provide temporary relief from that discomfort."

16. Why do some people repeat the same mistakes?

Good Answer:

"Some people repeat the same mistakes because they fail to reflect on their behavior and identify the root cause of the problem."

17. Why do some people give advice easily but fail to follow it themselves?

Good Answer:

"It is easier to think logically about someone else's situation than your own. Emotions often make it difficult to follow your own advice."

18. Why do some people keep making excuses?

Good Answer:

"People make excuses because excuses are easier than accepting responsibility. Excuses provide temporary emotional comfort."

19. Why do some people wait for the perfect time?

Good Answer:

"People wait for the perfect time because they want certainty before taking action. They fear making mistakes or facing failure."

20. Why do some people get offended by criticism?

Good Answer:

"Some people get offended because they see criticism as a personal attack rather than useful feedback."

21. Why do some people blame circumstances for everything?

Good Answer:

"Some people blame circumstances because accepting responsibility can be uncomfortable and threatening to their self-image."

22. Why do some people become jealous when others succeed?

Good Answer:

"People become jealous when they compare someone else's success to their own situation and feel left behind."

23. Why do some people refuse to change their opinion even when evidence proves they are wrong?

Good Answer:

"Some people refuse to change their opinion because admitting they are wrong threatens their ego and identity."

24. Why do some people constantly seek attention?

Good Answer:

"Some people seek attention because their self-worth depends heavily on recognition and validation from others."

25. Why do some people become obsessed with proving others wrong?

Good Answer:

"Some people become obsessed with proving others wrong because they tie their self-worth to being right."

26. Why do some people feel uncomfortable when someone else is more successful than they are?

Good Answer:

"They compare themselves to others and interpret another person's success as evidence of their own

subcategories.title: prompt
subcategories.content:
7 prompts that turn a profile into one recruiters cannot scroll past:

● The Headline Audit

"My current LinkedIn headline is: [paste]. I work in [industry] targeting [role type]. Generate 10 headline rewrites ranked most conservative to most aggressive. Each must communicate a specific result I delivered, not a job title. Show what makes each version work and which recruiter it appeals to."

✅ The first 220 characters decide whether a recruiter clicks. Most people fill them with the wrong information.

● The About Section Build

"Rewrite my About section as a 3-paragraph story. Paragraph 1: the specific problem I solve. Paragraph 2: proof, with numbers. Paragraph 3: who I want to hear from and how to reach me. Under 1,800 characters. First 2 lines must hook someone with 4 seconds."

✅ The About section is where you control the entire narrative. Most people use it to repeat their resume.

● The Experience Rewrite

"For each of my last 3 jobs, rewrite using: an opening sentence stating the business problem I owned, 3 bullets each with a measurable outcome, and a closing sentence on what changed because of my work. Replace every verb like 'managed' with one that shows ownership. Add numbers wherever the data supports them."

✅ Recruiters scan for numbers. No numbers, no callback.

● The Search Optimization Pass

"Pretend you're a senior recruiter searching LinkedIn for a [role] at a [company size/type]. List the exact 25 keywords you'd type into the search bar. Tell me which are missing from my profile, which sections to add them to, and how without keyword stuffing."

✅ LinkedIn is a search engine. Your profile either matches what recruiters type or it doesn't exist to them.

● The Brutal Review

"Read my full LinkedIn profile as a senior recruiter at [target company] with 50 other profiles to review. Give me the 30-second verdict: reach out, save, or skip? List the 5 things hurting the profile most and the 3 things that work. No softening."

✅ Most people have never seen their profile the way a recruiter actually does.

The last 2 prompts are in the comments.

LinkedIn Premium tells you who viewed your profile
Claude does the work that gets recruiters to view it

Save these.
If someone tells you LinkedIn is dead, send them this.

EG:1
Why do some people keep making excuses even when they know those excuses are hurting them?

People keep making excuses because excuses are easier than accepting responsibility. Taking responsibility forces people to face uncomfortable truths about their actions, decisions, or weaknesses. Excuses provide temporary emotional comfort, even when they prevent real change. As a result, people continue choosing short-term comfort over accountability.
